worth_it_explanation: Budget and pacing problems rarely surface in reporting until a flight has already been damaged. A campaign that exhausts its budget in the first two days stops delivering for the rest of the flight. A campaign set to 'Even' pacing that also carries tightly capped ad group budgets beneath it can stall for hours at a time as the ad group caps refill. Either pattern reduces impression volume, raises effective cost per result, and introduces gaps in frequency delivery. The checks here take a few minutes against the campaign and ad group settings in The Trade Desk platform and surface configuration conflicts before they affect a live flight.
intro: Pacing controls how The Trade Desk distributes a campaign's budget across the flight window. The two main options are 'Even' pacing, which spreads spend at a steady rate, and 'ASAP' pacing, which bids as aggressively as possible until the budget runs out. The choice between them depends on the campaign goal: brand reach and frequency generally need 'Even'; direct-response campaigns chasing scarce in-market signals may justify 'ASAP' for short bursts. A separate but related issue is the relationship between a campaign-level budget and the ad group budgets beneath it. In The Trade Desk's hierarchy, ad groups sit below campaigns and can carry their own daily or flight budgets. When those ad group caps are too small relative to the campaign budget, The Trade Desk cannot spend the campaign allocation even when inventory is available, and the campaign underpaces by design.

audit_checks.title: Pacing type does not match the campaign goal
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A brand awareness campaign set to 'ASAP' pacing burns through budget early in the day or flight, leaving no impression share for later windows when different audiences are active. A direct-response campaign set to 'Even' pacing may miss peak conversion windows by throttling spend at the moments when its audience is most available.

educate.base:
The Trade Desk's Kokai platform offers two pacing types at the campaign level: 'Even' and 'ASAP'. 'Even' pacing distributes budget at a roughly uniform rate across the flight window, which supports consistent reach, stable frequency, and predictable daily spend. 'ASAP' pacing bids on every eligible auction as quickly as possible until the daily or flight budget is exhausted, prioritizing speed of delivery over distribution.

The right choice depends on what the campaign is optimizing for. A brand awareness or connected television (CTV) campaign typically needs 'Even' pacing to reach audiences at different times of day and to hit frequency goals without burning through the budget before key dayparts. A retargeting campaign or a short-burst direct-response flight chasing a narrow in-market audience may benefit from 'ASAP' pacing since the audience pool is small and the value of each impression is tied to recency.

A few signs suggest the pacing type may be misaligned:
  • A brand campaign shows front-loaded delivery with near-zero impressions after mid-afternoon
  • A short direct-response flight is underspending across the day because 'Even' pacing is throttling bids below the clearing price at peak demand times
  • Koa's optimization suggestions reference pacing as a constraint on bid competitiveness
You may want to review the pacing setting alongside the delivery curve in The Trade Desk reporting before concluding the pacing type is the cause. Underspend can also result from targeting that is too narrow, bids that are too low, or ad group budget caps that are not large enough to absorb the campaign allocation.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an ecommerce campaign targeting cart abandoners or product page visitors, 'ASAP' pacing can make sense on short retargeting flights where the audience window is small and time-to-conversion matters. A longer brand or prospecting campaign on the same account should use 'Even' pacing to avoid burning inventory in the morning before peak shopping hours in the evening.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: Lead generation campaigns often see conversion rate variation by time of day, with lower rates late at night and higher rates during business hours or early evening. 'Even' pacing is the safer default here since it distributes budget across the day rather than spending it all in the first available hours. You may want to confirm whether the delivery curve aligns with the hours when the target audience is most likely to convert.

educate.learn_more_note: The Trade Desk maintains its documentation for this topic in its partner portal, which requires a logged-in seat and is not publicly accessible. No public reference is linked here. The Trade Desk's free Edge Academy provides public training on its platform, and practitioners with platform access can find the specific guidance in the in-platform Help Center.

investigate.intro: Review each campaign's pacing setting and compare it against the campaign goal and the delivery curve for the current or most recent flight.

the_trade_desk_ui.label: The Trade Desk UI
steps: Open the campaign in The Trade Desk platform and navigate to the campaign settings.
Locate the pacing field and note whether it is set to 'Even' or 'ASAP'. Note the campaign goal (brand awareness, reach, direct response, retargeting) and the flight dates.

Open the campaign's reporting view and pull delivery by hour or by day across the flight. Look for front-loaded delivery patterns that drop off sharply, which indicate that 'ASAP' pacing is exhausting the daily budget early.

For 'Even' pacing campaigns, check whether actual spend tracks the pacing target line or whether it falls short at specific times of day, which can indicate that the pacing setting is correct but bid prices or ad group caps are limiting delivery.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per campaign, the pacing setting, the stated campaign goal, and the delivery pattern from the reporting view. A campaign whose pacing type matches its goal and whose delivery curve is consistent with that goal passes. A brand campaign on 'ASAP' pacing with a front-loaded delivery curve, or a direct-response campaign on 'Even' pacing that misses its peak conversion windows, is a fail recorded with the campaign name and the specific pattern observed.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the pacing setting, campaign goal, and delivery curve observation for each campaign above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more campaigns on {{site_name}} are using a pacing type that does not align with the campaign goal. A brand or reach campaign set to 'ASAP' pacing will concentrate impressions in the earliest available inventory windows and leave later dayparts with no delivery, which disrupts frequency and reach. A direct-response campaign set to 'Even' pacing may throttle spend below the clearing price at the hours when the target audience is most active, leaving performance on the table. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the pacing setting for each affected campaign against its goal and flight dates, switching brand and reach campaigns to 'Even' pacing, and reserving 'ASAP' pacing for short direct-response or retargeting flights where recency and full-speed delivery are the priority.

audit_checks.title: Ad group budget caps are too low to absorb the campaign budget
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When ad group daily or flight budgets add up to less than the campaign budget, The Trade Desk cannot spend the campaign allocation even when inventory is available. The campaign shows underpacing in reporting, the flight ends with unspent budget, and the advertiser pays for a flight window that was never fully delivered.

educate.base:
In The Trade Desk's hierarchy, a campaign contains one or more ad groups, and each ad group can carry its own budget cap separate from the campaign-level budget. The campaign budget sets the maximum the advertiser is willing to spend across all ad groups for the flight. The ad group budgets set the maximum each individual ad group can spend.

When the ad group budgets sum to less than the campaign budget, a structural spending ceiling sits below the campaign allocation. The Trade Desk will not overspend an ad group's cap even if the campaign still has budget remaining and inventory is available. The result is a campaign that paces below its target and ends the flight with unspent funds.

This happens in a few configurations. A campaign may have been structured with conservative ad group caps that were never updated when the campaign budget increased. An ad group may have a daily cap that is appropriate early in the flight but becomes the bottleneck as competition rises later. Or a campaign may have started with several active ad groups, some of which were paused, leaving the remaining ad group caps insufficient to carry the full campaign budget.

A few signs suggest this configuration issue is present:
  • The campaign pacing report shows consistent underpacing below the target spend rate across multiple days
  • The campaign budget has remaining funds on the final day of the flight
  • The ad groups within the campaign each appear to hit their individual caps and stop delivering before the day ends
You may want to compare the sum of active ad group flight or daily budgets against the campaign budget before changing bids or targeting in response to underpacing.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a product catalog campaign split across multiple ad groups by product category, it is worth confirming that each ad group's budget reflects the expected spend share for that category. A high-margin product category ad group with a low cap will hit its limit early and stop competing for those product auctions even when the overall campaign has budget remaining.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead generation campaign split by audience segment, confirm that each ad group's budget is sufficient to deliver throughout the day rather than capping out by mid-morning. An ad group that exhausts its daily budget before peak lead-form conversion hours will not appear in afternoon auctions regardless of how much campaign budget remains.

educate.learn_more_note: The Trade Desk maintains its documentation for this topic in its partner portal, which requires a logged-in seat and is not publicly accessible. No public reference is linked here. The Trade Desk's free Edge Academy provides public training on its platform, and practitioners with platform access can find the specific guidance in the in-platform Help Center.

investigate.intro: Compare the sum of active ad group budgets against the campaign budget, and review the pacing report to determine whether individual ad group caps are stopping delivery before the campaign budget is reached.

the_trade_desk_ui.label: The Trade Desk UI
steps: Open the campaign in The Trade Desk platform and navigate to the ad groups tab. Note the budget and budget type (daily or flight) for each active ad group.

Sum the active ad group budgets and compare the total against the campaign budget. If the sum is lower than the campaign budget, there is a structural gap that prevents the campaign from spending its full allocation.

Open the campaign pacing report and look for consistent underpacing. If daily spend tracks closely against the sum of the ad group caps rather than the campaign budget, the ad group caps are the binding constraint.

Review each ad group's daily delivery chart. An ad group that stops delivering mid-day on most days and restarts at midnight is hitting its daily cap repeatedly, confirming the cap is below what the campaign pacing would require.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per campaign, the campaign budget, the sum of active ad group budgets, and whether the campaign pacing report shows consistent underpacing. A campaign whose active ad group budgets sum to at or above the campaign budget, and whose pacing report shows spend at or near the target rate, passes. A campaign whose active ad group budgets sum to less than the campaign budget, and whose pacing report shows consistent underpacing attributable to ad group caps hitting their limits, is a fail recorded with the campaign name, the budget gap amount, and the ad groups whose caps are the constraint.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the campaign budget, the sum of active ad group budgets, and the pacing report observation for each campaign above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more campaigns on {{site_name}} have ad group budget caps that sum to less than the campaign budget, which prevents The Trade Desk from spending the full campaign allocation even when qualifying inventory is available. The campaign pacing report shows consistent underspending, and the delivery pattern indicates that individual ad group caps are exhausted before the end of each day or the flight window. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the active ad group budgets within each affected campaign, raising the caps to match the expected spend share per ad group, and confirming that the updated sum meets or exceeds the campaign budget. After the change, monitor the pacing report for two to three days to confirm that spend now tracks closer to the campaign budget target.

audit_checks.title: Campaign ends the flight with material unspent budget
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Unspent budget at the end of a flight means the advertiser paid for a flight window they did not fully use. The missed impressions are not recoverable after the flight closes, and recurring underpacing across flights indicates a structural configuration problem rather than a one-time inventory shortage.

educate.base:
A campaign that ends its flight with unspent budget did not deliver what the advertiser planned and paid for. The Trade Desk's pacing system is designed to distribute budget across the flight window, and persistent underpacing signals that something in the campaign configuration is limiting delivery: the targeting is too narrow for the bid price, the bid price is too low to win auctions at the required rate, the ad group caps are insufficient, or the creative is not serving.

The distinction between occasional and recurring underpacing matters. A campaign that underpaces by a small percentage in one flight may reflect a short-term inventory shortage or a scheduling edge case. A campaign that consistently ends every flight 15 percent or more underspent points to a configuration issue that will repeat unless addressed.

The Trade Desk surfaces pacing status in the campaign list view with an indicator when a campaign is pacing behind target. Reviewing the indicator during active flights allows for mid-flight corrections rather than a post-mortem after the flight closes.

A few situations produce persistent underpacing worth investigating:
  • Bid prices that are below the market-clearing price for the targeted inventory and audience
  • Audience targeting that is too narrow to generate enough eligible auctions at the campaign's daily spend rate
  • Ad group budget caps, as described in the separate budget cap check, that prevent spend even when campaign budget remains
  • Creative rejections that reduce the number of eligible ad groups or ad formats serving
You may want to identify which of these factors is present before raising bids or loosening targeting. Each has a different fix.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an ecommerce retargeting campaign targeting recent site visitors, the audience pool shrinks as the flight progresses and earlier visitors age out of the lookback window. A campaign that paced normally in the first week but started underspending in the second may simply have fewer eligible users remaining. You may want to consider whether the flight duration matches the audience refresh rate.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead generation campaign with geographic or demographic targeting, persistent underspending can indicate that the defined audience is not large enough to sustain the daily spend rate across the flight. You may want to confirm whether expanding the audience, increasing the bid, or shortening the flight would better match the available inventory.

educate.learn_more_note: The Trade Desk maintains its documentation for this topic in its partner portal, which requires a logged-in seat and is not publicly accessible. No public reference is linked here. The Trade Desk's free Edge Academy provides public training on its platform, and practitioners with platform access can find the specific guidance in the in-platform Help Center.

investigate.intro: Review completed flights for unspent budget and identify the configuration factor limiting delivery.

the_trade_desk_ui.label: The Trade Desk UI
steps: Open the campaign list and filter for campaigns that ended in the past 90 days. Look for campaigns whose actual spend is materially below their flight budget.
For each underpacing campaign, open the reporting view and pull daily spend for the flight. Note whether underpacing was consistent across the flight or concentrated at the start or end.
Check the campaign's win rate in the reporting view. A low win rate alongside underpacing points to bid prices that are too low to win enough auctions at the required daily rate.

Confirm whether active ad group budget caps are a factor by comparing the sum of ad group budgets against the campaign budget, and whether any creative in the affected ad groups was rejected or paused during the flight.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per completed flight, the flight budget, the actual spend, the percentage of budget unspent, and whether the pattern recurred across multiple flights. A flight that spent 95 percent or more of its budget passes. A flight that ended with more than 5 percent unspent, and where the same campaign shows the same pattern in prior flights, is a fail recorded with the campaign name, the amount unspent, and the most likely contributing factor from the investigation steps above.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the flight budget, actual spend, and the investigation findings for each underpacing campaign above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more campaigns on {{site_name}} have consistently ended their flight windows with material unspent budget, indicating that the campaigns are not delivering at the rate the flight budget requires. Recurring underpacing across multiple flights points to a configuration issue rather than a one-time inventory gap. The most frequent causes are bid prices set below the market-clearing rate for the targeted inventory, audience targeting that is too narrow to generate enough eligible auctions, or ad group budget caps that prevent spend even when campaign budget remains. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the win rate, audience size, and ad group budget caps for each affected campaign, addressing the specific constraint the investigation identifies, and monitoring the pacing report for the next flight to confirm that delivery now tracks the budget target.
